course_info.title: Regional Studies: Middle East and Africa

class_schedule.topic: The Middle East and Africa: Defining and Understanding Two Complex Regions
learning_objectives: Analyze the colonial and post-colonial legacies shaping both regions
Identify key geographic, demographic, and resource dynamics
Evaluate how external intervention has shaped regional politics
readings: Cleveland & Bunton, A History of the Modern Middle East Ch. 1
Meredith, The Fate of Africa Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: The Middle East and Africa share colonial legacies, resource politics, and external intervention as common threads, while each has distinct historical trajectories.
key_concepts: Colonial legacies: arbitrary borders, ethnic divisions, institutional weakness
Oil and resource politics: the resource curse in both regions
Pan-Arabism and Pan-Africanism: failed unity movements
Cold War legacies and proxy conflicts
Demographic pressures: youth bulge, urbanization, migration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping exercise: tracing colonial borders and their relationship to ethnic, religious, and linguistic distributions

review.value: The 1916 secret British-French agreement dividing Ottoman territories into spheres of influence, creating borders that ignored ethnic and sectarian realities and whose consequences persist today
The 1884-85 Berlin Conference partition of Africa among European powers, creating artificial state borders that divided ethnic groups and combined hostile ones, seeding post-colonial conflicts

class_schedule.topic: The Arab-Israeli Conflict and the Palestinian Question
learning_objectives: Trace the history of the Arab-Israeli conflict from the Balfour Declaration to the present
Analyze the peace process and why it has stalled
Evaluate current dynamics: occupation, settlements, and international law

lecture_notes.introduction: The Arab-Israeli conflict is the longest-running geopolitical dispute in the modern Middle East and remains central to regional and global politics.
key_concepts: Zionism, the Balfour Declaration, and the British Mandate
1948 war, Palestinian displacement (Nakba), and the refugee question
1967 war, occupation, and UN Resolution 242
Oslo Accords: hopes and failures of the peace process
Current dynamics: settlements, blockade of Gaza, normalization agreements
lecture_notes.demonstration: Timeline and map analysis: territorial changes from 1947 partition plan to present

review.value: The 1967 UN Security Council resolution calling for Israeli withdrawal from occupied territories and the right of all states in the area to live in peace -- the foundation for decades of diplomacy, with key ambiguities still disputed
The 1993 agreements establishing Palestinian self-governance and a framework for permanent status negotiations, which ultimately failed to produce a final peace agreement

class_schedule.topic: Sectarianism, Political Islam, and Governance in the Middle East
learning_objectives: Analyze the Sunni-Shia divide and its political instrumentalization
Evaluate diverse manifestations of political Islam
Assess governance models: monarchies, republics, and failed states
readings: Gerges, Making the Arab World Ch. 1 (excerpt)
Lynch, The New Arab Wars Ch. 2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Sectarianism and political Islam are central dynamics in Middle Eastern politics, but they are far more complex than simplistic narratives suggest.
key_concepts: Sunni-Shia divide: historical origins and modern political instrumentalization
Political Islam: Muslim Brotherhood, Salafism, Iranian revolutionary model
Saudi-Iran rivalry as a geopolitical and sectarian proxy competition
Arab Spring: causes, trajectories, and outcomes
Failed states: Syria, Libya, Yemen -- causes and consequences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case comparison: divergent Arab Spring outcomes in Tunisia, Egypt, Syria, and Libya

review.value: The diverse movement to make Islamic principles the basis of political organization, ranging from democratic participation (Tunisia's Ennahda) to revolutionary theocracy (Iran) to jihadist violence (ISIS)
The geopolitical competition between Sunni-majority Saudi Arabia and Shia-majority Iran for regional influence, fought through proxy conflicts in Yemen, Syria, Lebanon, Iraq, and Bahrain

class_schedule.topic: Sub-Saharan Africa: Governance, Development, and Conflict
learning_objectives: Analyze governance challenges in sub-Saharan Africa
Evaluate the African Union's peace and security architecture
Assess Africa's development trajectory: obstacles and opportunities

lecture_notes.introduction: Sub-Saharan Africa is the most diverse region on Earth. Its challenges are real but so are its opportunities -- and outsiders consistently get the narrative wrong.
key_concepts: Governance diversity: from stable democracies (Botswana, Ghana) to fragile states (DRC, Somalia)
AU peace and security architecture: PSC, African Standby Force
Economic growth and structural transformation challenges
China-Africa relations: investment, debt, and influence
Youth bulge as both opportunity and risk
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data analysis: challenging stereotypes with African development success stories

review.value: The African Union's framework for conflict prevention and management, including the Peace and Security Council, Continental Early Warning System, and African Standby Force -- embodying 'African solutions for African problems'
The African Union's 50-year development vision for a prosperous, peaceful, and integrated Africa, organized around seven aspirations with specific targets and implementation plans

class_schedule.topic: Resource Politics: Oil, Minerals, and the Resource Curse
learning_objectives: Analyze the political economy of natural resources in the Middle East and Africa
Evaluate the resource curse thesis with regional evidence
Assess governance solutions: transparency, diversification, sovereign wealth funds

lecture_notes.introduction: Natural resources shape the politics and development of both regions profoundly -- often destructively. Understanding the resource-governance nexus is critical.
key_concepts: The resource curse: mechanisms (Dutch disease, rentier state, conflict financing)
Rentier states: how oil wealth undermines accountability and democracy
Conflict minerals: DRC cobalt, Sierra Leone diamonds, Liberian timber
Transparency initiatives: EITI, Kimberley Process, Dodd-Frank Section 1502
Diversification strategies: Saudi Vision 2030, Rwanda's services economy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative case study: Botswana (diamond success) vs. Nigeria (oil curse)

review.value: A state that derives a substantial portion of revenue from external rents (oil, minerals) rather than taxation, reducing the incentive to build accountable governance and economic diversification
When resource exports drive up the exchange rate, making non-resource exports uncompetitive and hollowing out the manufacturing and agriculture sectors

class_schedule.topic: Contemporary Dynamics: Great Power Competition and Regional Agency
learning_objectives: Analyze US, Chinese, Russian, and Turkish roles in both regions
Evaluate the concept of regional agency: how local actors shape external involvement
Assess future scenarios for both regions
readings: Abdi, Africa in World Politics Ch. 10 (excerpt)
Hokayem, Iran's Foreign Policy: From Defiance to Accommodation (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: The Middle East and Africa are not merely objects of great power competition. Regional actors exercise significant agency in shaping their own futures.
key_concepts: US retrenchment and its consequences for both regions
China's expanding footprint: BRI, military base in Djibouti, economic partnerships
Russia's return: Wagner Group, arms sales, political influence
Turkey and the UAE as emerging regional powers
African and Middle Eastern agency: how local actors leverage external competition
lecture_notes.demonstration: Great power competition mapping: external actor interests and influence in a selected country

review.value: The capacity of local and regional actors to shape, resist, and leverage external interventions rather than simply being passive recipients of great power competition
The 2020 normalization agreements between Israel and the UAE, Bahrain, Morocco, and Sudan, reshaping Middle Eastern alignments by prioritizing shared interests over Palestinian solidarity

quizzes.topic: Middle East Politics and the Arab-Israeli Conflict

questions.question: What are the Sykes-Picot borders and why do scholars argue they are a root cause of instability in the modern Middle East?
questions.answer: The Sykes-Picot Agreement (1916) was a secret British-French deal to divide the Ottoman Empire's Arab territories into spheres of influence after WWI. The resulting borders created states (Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan) that grouped together diverse and sometimes hostile ethnic and sectarian communities (Sunnis, Shia, Kurds, Christians, Druze) without regard for local identities. These artificial borders created states where no natural political community existed, producing governance challenges: ruling elites often relied on sectarian or tribal patronage rather than inclusive institutions. Many argue this is a root cause of instability because: state legitimacy remains weak; identity politics follows sectarian rather than national lines; and when authoritarian rulers fall (as in Iraq 2003, Syria 2011), the underlying divisions erupt into conflict.

rubric.key: Connects to instability with specific mechanisms

questions.question: Explain the difference between the Arab Spring outcomes in Tunisia and Egypt. What factors explain why Tunisia transitioned to democracy while Egypt reverted to authoritarianism?
questions.answer: Tunisia successfully transitioned to democracy after its 2011 revolution, adopting a democratic constitution in 2014 and holding free elections. Egypt initially elected Mohamed Morsi (Muslim Brotherhood) but reverted to military authoritarianism after the 2013 coup by Abdel Fattah el-Sisi. Key explanatory factors: (1) Civil society: Tunisia had a strong labor union (UGTT) and civil society that mediated between Islamists and secularists (winning the Nobel Peace Prize); Egypt's civil society was weaker and more polarized. (2) Military role: Tunisia's military was small and apolitical; Egypt's military is a political and economic powerhouse unwilling to surrender power. (3) Political compromise: Tunisia's Ennahda party agreed to share power and compromise; in Egypt, the Brotherhood and secularists refused compromise. (4) Homogeneity: Tunisia is relatively homogeneous; Egypt's larger, more diverse society created deeper divisions.

quizzes.topic: Africa, Resource Politics, and External Actors

questions.question: Explain the concept of the 'rentier state' and how it applies to oil-producing countries in the Middle East. How does it differ from the resource curse in mineral-rich African states?
questions.answer: A rentier state derives most revenue from external rents (oil royalties, fees) rather than taxing its citizens. This breaks the accountability link between taxation and representation: because the government does not need citizens' money, it faces less pressure to be responsive or transparent. In the Gulf states (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Kuwait), oil wealth funds generous social services that buy legitimacy without democratic accountability. The resource curse in mineral-rich African states (DRC, Sierra Leone) operates differently: minerals are often geographically concentrated and artisanally mined, enabling armed groups to capture resource revenues and finance conflict. Weak state institutions cannot control extraction zones, leading to violence rather than the stable authoritarianism of Gulf rentier states. Both share the mechanisms of Dutch disease and institutional weakness, but the political outcomes differ based on state capacity and resource type.

rubric.key: Explains the difference in African mineral contexts

questions.question: How is China's engagement in Africa different from traditional Western aid and investment? Identify one benefit and one concern for African states.
questions.answer: China's engagement differs in several ways: it combines aid, trade, investment, and loans in integrated packages rather than separating them; it imposes no political conditionality (no demands for democracy or governance reform); it focuses on infrastructure (roads, railways, ports, telecommunications) rather than social sectors; and it uses tied aid requiring Chinese contractors and workers. Benefit: Chinese investment fills a massive infrastructure gap that Western donors have neglected, and the absence of conditionality respects sovereignty and allows faster project implementation. Concern: debt sustainability -- some countries (Zambia, Sri Lanka, Laos) have taken on unsustainable Chinese loans, raising fears of 'debt-trap diplomacy' where defaulting countries must surrender strategic assets or political leverage, though evidence of deliberate debt traps is debated.

rubric.key: Identifies key differences from Western engagement

major_assignments.title: Regional Comparative Analysis Paper
major_assignments.description: Select a cross-cutting theme (governance, resource politics, identity, external intervention) and compare its manifestation in one Middle Eastern and one African country.
objectives: Apply comparative methods to diverse contexts
Analyze how shared themes produce different outcomes in different contexts

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on regional politics, history, and current events

participation.description: Engagement in discussions, simulations, and mapping exercises

major_assignments.description: Cross-regional comparative analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam on Middle Eastern and African politics and history

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam with scenario-based regional analysis

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. Simulation absences cannot be made up.
course_policies.collaboration: Simulations collaborative. Written work individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original. Proper citation required.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to regional databases, media sources in translation, and academic journals.
